Name: informationtheoreticmodelcompression
Title: Information-Theoretic Framework for Adaptive Model Compression
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adaptive model compression framework, grounded in rate-distortion theory, can dynamically allocate bits to neural network parameters based on their task-relevant information content, leading to more efficient and accurate models.
Related Work: Existing work in model compression, such as [1] and [2], has explored the use of rate-distortion theory to understand and optimize the compression of neural networks. However, these approaches often focus on static pruning and quantization strategies that do not adapt to the specific information content of the model during inference. Our proposal aims to extend these ideas by developing a dynamic, information-theoretic framework that adapts compression based on the real-time information content of the activations, providing a more fine-grained and context-aware approach.
Abstract: We propose an information-theoretic framework for adaptive model compression that dynamically allocates bits to neural network parameters based on their task-relevant information content. By treating the compression process as a rate-distortion problem, we aim to optimize the trade-off between the compression ratio and the model's performance. Our framework computes the mutual information between the activations and the task output, using this to guide the allocation of bits. This adaptive approach ensures that the model remains efficient while maintaining or even improving its accuracy. We validate our framework through experiments on image classification and language modeling tasks, demonstrating superior performance compared to static compression methods.

Experiments.Description: Evaluate the mutual information between activations and the task output using InfoNCE estimators. This will provide a measure of the information content of each layer's activations.
Experiments.Metrics: Mutual Information (MI) between activations and task output

Experiments.Description: Develop a dynamic bit allocation strategy based on the computed mutual information. Allocate more bits to layers with higher MI and fewer bits to layers with lower MI.
Experiments.Metrics: Bits allocated to each layer, compression ratio, and model accuracy

Experiments.Description: Compare the performance of the adaptive compression framework to static compression methods (e.g., uniform quantization, static pruning) on image classification tasks (CIFAR-10, ImageNet) and language modeling tasks (Wikitext-2, PTB).
Experiments.Metrics: Accuracy, compression ratio, and computational efficiency

Experiments.Description: Analyze the impact of different mutual information thresholds on the performance and compression efficiency. Explore the trade-off between compression ratio and model accuracy.
Experiments.Metrics: Compression ratio, accuracy, and computational efficiency

Risk Factors and Limitations: The computation of mutual information can be computationally expensive, especially for large models. Efficient estimators and approximations may be necessary to make the framework practical.

The dynamic bit allocation strategy may introduce additional complexity and overhead during inference. Careful optimization and benchmarking will be required to ensure that the benefits outweigh the costs.
The framework assumes that the mutual information is a reliable proxy for the task-relevant information content. This assumption may not hold in all cases, and further validation is needed.
